title: St. Timothy of Alexandria, St. Paphnutius, St. Stephen of Antioch & St. Martinianus
narrative:
St. Timothy Pope of Alexandria, St. Paphnutius the Confessor, St. Stephen of Antioch, and St. Martinianus of Caesarea

On the thirteenth of Yekatit, the holy Church sets before the faithful four witnesses whose lives are bound together by the thread of patient endurance in the face of institutional hostility — bishops and monks who faced the fury not of Roman emperors but of theological controversy and ecclesiastical politics, and who maintained the faith with a steadiness that outlasted every assault. Saint Timothy the Second, Pope of Alexandria, inherited one of the most turbulent patriarchates of the fifth century, and governed it during the period immediately following the Council of Chalcedon, when the Oriental churches were being torn apart by the question of whether to receive or reject that council's definition of Christ's nature. Timothy upheld the Miaphysite confession of Alexandria with firmness and pastoral wisdom, and his tenure is remembered as one in which the Alexandrian church maintained both its theological integrity and its internal unity under enormous external pressure.

Saint Paphnutius the Confessor is a more ancient figure, a desert bishop of Egypt who attended the Council of Nicaea with one eye blinded and one knee hamstrung from the tortures of the Diocletianic persecution. The tradition records that Constantine, when he saw Paphnutius in the council chamber, came down from his throne and kissed the empty eye socket as a relic of Christ's suffering. Paphnutius is remembered not only for his wounds but for a remarkable intervention at Nicaea: when the proposal was made to forbid married men from living with their wives after ordination, it was Paphnutius — himself a celibate monk — who rose and argued against the measure, saying that it was too heavy a burden to lay upon the clergy. His voice for moderation, coming from one who practised the most rigorous asceticism, carried the day.

Saint Stephen of Antioch is among the lesser-known witnesses of this assembly — a patriarch of Antioch who governed that venerable see during the turbulent period of the Acacian Schism, when the Eastern and Western churches were divided and the patriarchs of the East were under pressure from Constantinople to modify their Christological positions. Stephen maintained the Oriental confession with courage and was eventually deposed and exiled, ending his days in a distant see, faithful to the end to the tradition he had received. The Ethiopian Church commemorates him as a confessor of the patriarchal dignity — one who lost his throne rather than compromise the teaching of his fathers.

Saint Martinianus of Caesarea in Palestine is this day's figure of discovery and one of the most psychologically acute stories in the entire Synaxarium tradition. Martinianus was a hermit of great repute whose solitude was invaded by a woman who, having heard of his holiness, disguised herself as a beggar to gain admittance to his cell, intending to seduce him. Finding himself in danger of falling, Martinianus did not drive her out — he stepped into the fire of his own hearth, standing in the flames until the pain destroyed the temptation. The woman, witnessing this act of violent self-mastery, was herself converted on the spot, wept bitterly, and eventually became a great ascetic in her own right on the island of Patras. Martinianus healed of his burns and continued his life of prayer. The tradition does not present his act as a model to be imitated but as a sign of what the love of God, when it has truly taken hold of a person, can produce — a freedom from the fear of physical pain that makes possible the most extraordinary acts of self-overcoming. Amen.
